intro-self-001.answer: I am a senior Android engineer with experience in large consumer apps, modularization, performance optimization, and cross-team delivery. My strongest project was leading the modularization and startup performance work for a super app where I owned architecture review, migration milestones, and release quality gates.
follow_up_answers: I personally owned the Android module boundary design, startup optimization roadmap, and the release quality gate definition with QA and backend partners.
The project reduced cold start latency by 28 percent, lowered ANR rate, and improved release confidence through measurable rollout gates.

screening-auth-001.answer: I led the architecture review for our Android super app and owned the evaluation of startup, ANR, and release stability risks before we started the modularization program.
follow_up_answers: My direct responsibility was aligning Android module boundaries and defining the migration milestones with backend and QA.
We measured startup latency, ANR rate, and crash free users before and after the first migration milestone.

round1-core-001.answer: For lifecycle-heavy features I always explain state transitions, background restrictions, and how we avoid leaking work across Activity recreation by moving state into ViewModel and clearly separating UI effects from persistent state.
follow_up_answers: A common failure mode was duplicate requests after recreation, so I introduced idempotent events and lifecycle-aware collectors.
I used logs, traces, and targeted reproduction steps to confirm that the fix removed duplicate work.

round2-arch-001.answer: I designed the module boundaries by splitting startup-critical code from optional features, keeping dependency edges obvious, and moving risky initialization behind lazy gates. That let us protect cold start while we migrated gradually.
follow_up_answers: The key tradeoff was keeping the architecture clean without turning the migration into a full rewrite, so I chose a phased boundary plan with explicit milestones.
We monitored startup latency, ANR, and crash free users on every rollout gate, and we kept a rollback switch ready if the signals moved the wrong way.

round2-perf-lite-001.answer: I found the startup bottleneck with trace data and then removed one expensive main-thread initialization path. That gave us a safer improvement without overengineering the whole app.
follow_up_answers: The measurement that pointed us there was startup trace visibility on the critical path.
We protected the change with regression monitoring and a staged rollout.

round2-deep-dive-001.answer: I would defend the phased startup strategy because a rewrite would have delayed the release window and increased migration risk. The safer choice was to keep the critical path stable, then improve the rest through smaller checkpoints.
follow_up_answers: I rejected the rewrite because it would have made rollback harder and introduced too many unknowns in one release.
If I restarted today, I would invest earlier in dependency visibility and migration dashboards so the phased plan would be even easier to steer.

round3-biz-001.answer: In one project we changed the Android technical plan because the business needed a faster regional launch. I kept the core architecture but reduced the initial scope, isolated risky integrations behind feature flags, and aligned product, backend, and QA on a phased delivery plan.
follow_up_answers: The business constraint was a fixed market launch date tied to a campaign, so the cost of missing the window was higher than shipping every planned capability in the first release.
I kept engineering risk under control by cutting low-confidence scope, protecting critical paths with monitoring, and keeping a rollback strategy for the riskiest integrations.

hr-lead-001.answer: As a TL candidate I focus on team health, delivery clarity, and stakeholder alignment. In difficult situations I first clarify goals, identify the real constraint, and then align the team on a smaller plan we can execute without losing quality.
follow_up_answers: One conflict happened when product pushed an aggressive delivery date, so I re-scoped the release and explained the risk tradeoff with concrete quality metrics.
The lesson was that early alignment on priorities prevents late-stage conflict and reduces churn.
